title: Good Green Jobs in a Global Economy: Making and Keeping New Industries in the United States By David J. Hess MIT Press. 2012. 312 pages. $30 hardcover, $20 paperback
abstract: Acentral debate in environmental sociology revolves around the degree of the disjuncture between currently existing social-environmental dynamics and the social arrangements necessary to achieve environmental sustainability. One school of thought, ecological modernization theory, argues that this tension is a relatively minor issue, as evolutionary corrections in the form of technological innovation and increases in efficiency will lead to a decoupling of the environment and economy. Other approaches emphasize the “enduring conflict” between the environment and society that explains the political difficulties preventing a fundamental restructuring of social-environmental dynamics (Schnaiberg and Gould 2000). Global-scale quantitative assessments of the competing views generally support the claims of the more critical analytical approaches (York, Rosa, and Dietz 2003; York and Rosa 2003). David J. Hess's book Good Green Jobs in a Global Economy: Making and Keeping New Industries in the United States attempts to chart a path between the bright optimism of ecological modernizationists and the politically unfeasible (though perhaps environmentally necessary) calls for radically transforming the global economy. Instead of viewing sustainability as a binary category, he focuses on the transition to a green economy through a focus on recent trends in energy policy.
